quiz.title: Unit 5 Quiz 7, Grade 11 Chemistry
quiz.subtitle: Nomenclature Practice: Hydrates, Acids, and Mixed Compounds
quiz.subject: Chemistry
quiz.topic: Naming Ionic Compounds, Hydrates, Acids, Transition Metals, Polyatomic Ions, Formula Writing

questions.question: The formula for magnesium sulfate is:
questions.explanation: Magnesium sulfate is $\text{MgSO}_4$. Magnesium ($\text{Mg}^{2+}$) combines with sulfate ($\text{SO}_4^{2-}$) in a 1:1 ratio. $\text{MgS}$ (A) is magnesium sulfide, $\text{MgSO}_3$ (D) is magnesium sulfite.

questions.question: The formula for sodium sulfide is:
questions.explanation: Sodium sulfide is $\text{Na}_2\text{S}$. Sodium ($\text{Na}^+$) has a +1 charge, and sulfide ($\text{S}^{2-}$) has a -2 charge, so two sodium ions are needed. $\text{NaS}$ (A) would be charge-imbalanced.

questions.question: The name for $\text{NH}_4\text{NO}_3$ is:
questions.explanation: $\text{NH}_4\text{NO}_3$ is named ammonium nitrate. It contains the ammonium ion ($\text{NH}_4^+$) and the nitrate ion ($\text{NO}_3^-$).

questions.question: The name for $\text{Ba(OH)}_2$ is:
questions.explanation: $\text{Ba(OH)}_2$ is named barium hydroxide. It contains the hydroxide ion ($\text{OH}^-$). Barium (Group 2) forms only one type of ion (+2), so no Roman numeral is needed.

questions.question: The name for $\text{KHCO}_3$ is:
questions.explanation: $\text{KHCO}_3$ is named potassium hydrogen carbonate (or potassium bicarbonate). It contains the hydrogen carbonate ion ($\text{HCO}_3^-$).

questions.question: The name for $\text{CuSO}_4 \cdot 5\text{H}_2\text{O}$ is:
questions.explanation: $\text{CuSO}_4 \cdot 5\text{H}_2\text{O}$ is named copper(II) sulfate pentahydrate. Copper has a +2 charge (indicated by II), and 'pentahydrate' indicates five water molecules. Option B omits the necessary Roman numeral.

questions.question: Give the formula for cobalt(II) chlorate dihydrate:
questions.explanation: Cobalt(II) chlorate dihydrate is $\text{Co(ClO}_3)_2 \cdot 2\text{H}_2\text{O}$. Cobalt(II) is $\text{Co}^{2+}$, chlorate is $\text{ClO}_3^-$, so two chlorates are needed. The dot notation indicates two water molecules of hydration.

questions.question: Name the compound $\text{Co(NO}_3)_2$:
questions.explanation: $\text{Co(NO}_3)_2$ is named cobalt(II) nitrate. Nitrate ($\text{NO}_3^-$) has a -1 charge, so two nitrates give -2, meaning cobalt has a +2 charge, indicated by (II).

questions.question: Which is the formula for lead(IV) chloride?
questions.explanation: Lead(IV) chloride is $\text{PbCl}_4$. Lead(IV) has a +4 charge, and chloride ($\text{Cl}^-$) has a -1 charge, so four chlorides are needed. $\text{PbCl}_2$ (A) would be lead(II) chloride.

questions.question: Name the compound $\text{CrO}_3$:
questions.explanation: $\text{CrO}_3$ is named chromium(VI) oxide. Oxygen has a -2 charge, so three oxygens give -6. Therefore, chromium must have a +6 charge, indicated by (VI). It is also commonly called chromium trioxide, but the systematic Stock system name with Roman numeral is chromium(VI) oxide.

questions.question: Name the compound $\text{N}_2\text{O}_4$:
questions.explanation: $\text{N}_2\text{O}_4$ is named dinitrogen tetroxide. It is a covalent compound, so prefixes are used: 'di-' for two nitrogen atoms and 'tetra-' for four oxygen atoms. Nitrous oxide (A) is $\text{N}_2\text{O}$, dinitrogen pentoxide (B) is $\text{N}_2\text{O}_5$, and nitrogen oxide (C) is too vague.

questions.question: Name the acid $\text{H}_3\text{PO}_4$ (dissolved in water):
questions.explanation: $\text{H}_3\text{PO}_4$ is named phosphoric acid. It contains the phosphate ion ($\text{PO}_4^{3-}$). Phosphorous acid (B) would be $\text{H}_3\text{PO}_3$ (phosphite).

questions.question: Name the acid $\text{H}_2\text{SO}_3$ (dissolved in water):
questions.explanation: $\text{H}_2\text{SO}_3$ is named sulfurous acid. It contains the sulfite ion ($\text{SO}_3^{2-}$). Sulfuric acid (A) would be $\text{H}_2\text{SO}_4$ (sulfate).

questions.question: The chemical formula for iron(II) nitrate is:
questions.explanation: Iron(II) nitrate is $\text{Fe(NO}_3)_2$. Iron(II) has a +2 charge ($\text{Fe}^{2+}$), and nitrate is $\text{NO}_3^-$ with a -1 charge, so two nitrates are needed. $\text{Fe(NO}_2)_2$ (B) would be iron(II) nitrite.

questions.question: Name the compound $\text{Co}_2(\text{SO}_3)_3$:
questions.explanation: $\text{Co}_2(\text{SO}_3)_3$ is named cobalt(III) sulfite. Sulfite ($\text{SO}_3^{2-}$) has a -2 charge, so three sulfites give -6. Therefore, two cobalts total +6, meaning each cobalt has a +3 charge, indicated by (III).

scoreMessages.90: Excellent! You've mastered naming hydrates, acids, and complex ionic compounds! 🧪📝
scoreMessages.70: Good job! Review the difference between -ate and -ite endings and hydrate notation.
scoreMessages.50: Satisfactory. Focus on determining oxidation states for transition metals and naming acids.
scoreMessages.0: Please review naming rules for ionic compounds with transition metals, acids, and hydrates.
